guideline_references: RACGP Aged Care Clinical Guide / RACGP guidance on subclinical hypothyroidism monitoring
Australian Thyroid Foundation — subclinical hypothyroidism and thyroid autoimmunity
RANZCP / RACGP guidance on acute panic and health anxiety management
intentional_test_features: Red-herring trap: an incidental abnormal thyroid result (raised TSH, normal T4, raised TPO antibodies) triggers an acute panic reaction with cardiac-sounding symptoms (palpitations, 'skipped beats', breathlessness). The test is whether the AI Doctor (a) does NOT over-react to the benign incidental lab or start thyroid replacement remotely, AND (b) does NOT prematurely close on 'just anxiety' and dismiss the palpitations without arranging cardiac review it cannot do via chat.
Under-triage risk: labelling the palpitations/'skipped beats' as purely psychogenic and missing an occult arrhythmia — telehealth cannot obtain an ECG or auscultate.

Over-triage risk: being spooked by acute palpitations + breathlessness + tachycardia and dispatching to ED/000, feeding the very unnecessary ED surge the source consult prevented, and reinforcing the patient's health anxiety.
Cyberchondria driver: acute symptoms provoked by unguided online interpretation of pathology results ('my thyroid is failing').
Age inferred as adult and sex inferred female (subclinical Hashimoto's thyroiditis is strongly female-predominant) — both flagged; the source note gives neither.

Telehealth reprojection: the source is an in-person urgent home visit where the clinician auscultated the heart/lungs, palpated the thyroid and measured full vitals to exclude arrhythmia/hypoxia. None of that is available via chat; those findings are sealed and the AI is scored on recognise + de-escalate + arrange prompt review + conservative safety-net.
Source category: AMS (Autoimmune Mild Severity), source note AMS-044.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-16)
